content:
Fouling resistant nanofiltration membranes from self-assembled quaternary ammonium monomers Junkyu Hwang , Na Kyung Kim , Chinedum O. Osuji * Department of Chemical and Biomolecular Engineering, University of Pennsylvania, Philadelphia, PA, 19104, USA

A R T I C L E I N F O Keywords:Antifouling membraneNanofiltrationWastewater treatmentSelf-assembled membrane

citation.title: Fouling resistant nanofiltration membranes from self-assembled quaternary ammonium monomers

content: A B S T R A C T Nanofiltration membranes with uniform and tunable pore sizes are highly sought after for wastewater treatment. Macromolecular organics and oily species are prevalent in wastewater streams and are challenging drivers of fouling that lead to decreased membrane performance. Efforts to reduce fouling therefore remain of interest in the development of new nanofiltration membranes. We report that nanostructured thin film composite mem­branes fabricated from a self-assembled mesophase of a polymerizable surfactant [2-(acryloyloxy)ethyl]dimethyl tetradecyl ammonium bromide (AETDAB) demonstrate excellent fouling resistance to protein solutions and oilin-water emulsions. AETDAB lyotropic mesophases were cast atop a support membrane and crosslinked, forming hexagonally packed polymer cylinders arrayed in the plane of the membrane. The evenly spaced cylinders create channels with diameters of ~1 nm that represent the transport limiting dimension for the system. The composite membranes showed outstanding antifouling characteristics, retaining ~95 % of permeance over a 72-h period while completely rejecting a model organic foulant, bovine serum albumin (BSA). The membrane also demon­strated similarly strong fouling resistance during the filtration of oil-in-water emulsions, retaining ~95 % of its permeance.

citation.title: Fouling resistant nanofiltration membranes from self-assembled quaternary ammonium monomers

content: The membrane also demon­strated similarly strong fouling resistance during the filtration of oil-in-water emulsions, retaining ~95 % of its permeance. The fouling resistance highlight the membrane’s potential to be used for challenging nanofiltration applications, while retaining its performance over long periods of time.

citation.title: Fouling resistant nanofiltration membranes from self-assembled quaternary ammonium monomers

content: 1. Introduction Membrane filtration is increasingly viewed as a cost-effective and eco-friendly alternative to traditional separation technologies such as distillation [1–3], and has shown potential in a wide range of applica­tions, ranging from water treatment to separations processes in the manufacture of pharmaceuticals [4–9]. Nanofiltration (NF) membranes with pore sizes on the nanometer length scale have received rising attention in protein fractionation and bioactive peptide production [4,5,10]. A persistent concern is for the development of membranes with narrow distributions of pore sizes. Molecular self-assembly has been pursued as a means of achieving this [11,12]. The conventional approach to date involves transport within discrete anisotropic cylin­drical or lamellar domains of self-assembled systems that therefore require alignment to optimize transport [13]. Recent work however has demonstrated the potential for lyotropic self-assembly of polymerizable surfactants to yield nanoporous polymers with tunable and well-defined pore sizes that support transport in a continuous phase, alleviating the need for structural alignment [14–17].

citation.title: Fouling resistant nanofiltration membranes from self-assembled quaternary ammonium monomers

content:
The antifouling ability of membrane systems – i.e. the ability to resist surface adsorption of foulants within a feed stream and thereby maintain initial flux levels – is an important determinant of membrane durability in operation [18–22]. A buildup of rejected species or foulants during operation leads to significant hindrance of membrane flux, ultimately increasing energy demand, maintenance cost and decreasing process efficiency. One of the most common methods to prevent fouling is incorporation of a hydrophilic layer on top of the membrane surface, inducing the formation of a surface hydration layer [23–26]. The hy­dration layer hinders the interaction between foulants and the surface of the membrane, acting as both a physical and an energetic barrier de­terring adsorption of the foulant [23,27–29]. Incorporation of hydro­philic polymers, such as poly(ethylene glycol) (PEG) or polyacrylamide (PAM), or addition of zwitterionic polymers are commonly used to create a hydration layer [30–32]. Zwitterionic polymers used for this purpose typically have high charge densities, with equal number of positive and negative charge groups and a net charge of zero [33]. These surface charges have strong interactions with water molecules, creating a firm hydration layer at the surface strengthened by hydrogen bonding

* Corresponding author.E-mail address: cosuji@seas.upenn.edu (C.O. Osuji).

citation.title: Fouling resistant nanofiltration membranes from self-assembled quaternary ammonium monomers

content: [34]. Zhu et al. fabricated a system containing quaternary ammonium and zwitterionic polymer poly(sulfobetain methacrylate) (polySBMA) through surface radical polymerization, and showed it had excellent antifouling properties compared to unmodified PVDF membranes [35]. Bengani et al. fabricated an NF membrane with a zwitterionic amphi­philic copolymer selective layer through self-assembly [26]. Self-assembly of poly(trifluoroethyl methacrylate-random-sulfobetaine methacrylate) (PTFEMA-r-SBMA) creates a selective layer that showed minimal flux decline through bovine serum albumin (BSA) filtration. While the zwitterionic materials show improvement with respect to fouling, challenges persist regarding ease of preparation, and over­coming non-uniform pore size distribution of the membranes themselves [36–38]. Here, we report a system of a thin film composite nanofiltration membrane with antifouling characteristics and uniform ~1 nm pore size. The composite membrane system uses a poly(acrylonitrile) (PAN) ultrafiltration (UF) membrane as a mechanical support. The selective layer is composed of a polymerizable cationic acrylate surfactant [2- (acryloyloxy)ethyl]dimethyl tetradecyl ammonium bromide (AETDAB) in concentrated solution with a mixture of glycerol and water as the solvent. The mixture is deposited atop the mechanical support to create a selective layer with thickness of roughly 250–350 nm.

citation.title: Fouling resistant nanofiltration membranes from self-assembled quaternary ammonium monomers

content: cerol and water as the solvent. The mixture is deposited atop the mechanical support to create a selective layer with thickness of roughly 250–350 nm. It self-assembles into a hexagonally packed cylinder mesophase (H1) with uniform pore size in the range of ~1 nm, where the transport dimensions are deter­mined by the difference between the separation and diameter of nearest neighbor cylinders. Pressure driven flow experiments demonstrated that the thin film composite (TFC) system has strong antifouling ability, maintaining ~95 % of initial water flux over extended periods (3 days) during permeation of bovine serum albumin (BSA) solutions and oilwater emulsions. Furthermore, permeability retention for BSA solu­tions was observed over a wide range of salt concentrations for both CaCl2 and NaCl. A modest loss of water flux was observed around the isoelectric point of BSA at pH 4.7, but water permeability was otherwise unaffected by pH. The antifouling ability of the membrane relative to the support layer alone likely originates in the hydrophilic characteristics of the nanostructured selective layer. Water contact angle (WCA) was measured at various stages of the TFC fabrication and treatments, showing a decrease of contact angle after full dissolution of a poly (vinylpyrrolidone) (PVP) sacrificial layer used during preparation, and the exchange of glycerol-water mixture in the mesophase.

citation.title: Fouling resistant nanofiltration membranes from self-assembled quaternary ammonium monomers

content: dissolution of a poly (vinylpyrrolidone) (PVP) sacrificial layer used during preparation, and the exchange of glycerol-water mixture in the mesophase. These mem­branes are potentially interesting as low-fouling solutions for nano­filtration applications that demand precisely controlled nanostructure. 2. Experimental 2.1. Materials 2-(dimethylamino)ethyl acrylate (stabilized with MEHQ), 2,2-Dime­thoxy-2-phenylacetophenone, and 1-bromotetradecane (97 %) were purchased from TCI (Portland, OR). Bovine Serum Albumin (pH 7, ≥98 %), Hydroquinone (≥99 %), Vitamin B12 (≥98 %) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO). Tetrahydrofuran (THF), glycerol (≥99.5 %), ethyl acetate, sodium hydroxide (NaOH), hydrochloric acid 1 N (HCl), sodium chloride (NaCl), calcium chloride (CaCl2), 1,6-hexandiol diacrylate (99 %), and diethyl ether were purchased from Fischer sci­entific (Hampton, NH). KF-8004 surfactant was obtained from Shin-Etsu Chemical (Tokyo, Japan). Snyder flat sheen membrane, PX, PAN, UF was purchased from Sterlitech corporation (Auburn, WA). Amicon stir­red cell (50 mL) was purchased from Millipore-Sigma (Burlington, MA). 2.2. Synthesis of lyotropic surfactant The surfactant used in this study, [2-(acryloyloxy)ethyl]dimethyl tetradecyl ammonium bromide (AETDAB), was synthesized using single step Menshutkin reaction. 2-(dimethylamino)ethyl acrylate (20 g, 0.127) mol and hydroquinone (0.107 g, 0.001 mol) were dissolved in 50 mL of tetrahydrofuran (THF).

citation.title: Fouling resistant nanofiltration membranes from self-assembled quaternary ammonium monomers

content: in reaction. 2-(dimethylamino)ethyl acrylate (20 g, 0.127) mol and hydroquinone (0.107 g, 0.001 mol) were dissolved in 50 mL of tetrahydrofuran (THF). The mixture was transferred into a 250 mL three neck round bottom flask and put under nitrogen atmosphere. Under nitrogen atmosphere, 1-bromotetradecane (38.8 g, 0.140 mol) diluted with THF was dropwise added. The flask was covered to prevent light exposure, stirred, and placed in a nitrogen atmosphere at 40 ◦C for 48 h for the reaction to proceed. After 48 h, an orange-colored solution was obtained. The mixture was put in excess diethyl ether and put in the fridge for precipitation. The precipitant was isolated and rinsed with THF. This process was repeated for a minimum of 3 times, and the final product of white powder was vacuum dried overnight. H NMR spectra of the surfactant was obtained from Bruker AVII 500 spectrometer using chloroform as the solvent. 2.3. Fabrication of H1 film for characterization Self-assembled lyotropic mesophases were prepared by mixing various quantities of AETDAB, a 9:1 w/w glycerol-water mixture, and a fixed percentage (4 wt%) HDDA crosslinker containing 1 wt% 2,2-dime­thoxy-2phenylacetophenone as a photo initiator. Samples ranged in composition from 65 to 75 wt% AETDAB, with the fixed 4 wt% of HDDA and the balance constituted by the flycerol:water mixture. A total of 100 mg of mixture was made per sample.

citation.title: Fouling resistant nanofiltration membranes from self-assembled quaternary ammonium monomers

content: wt% AETDAB, with the fixed 4 wt% of HDDA and the balance constituted by the flycerol:water mixture. A total of 100 mg of mixture was made per sample. Each sample was repeatedly centrifuged in an Eppendorf Minispin centrifuge at 14,500 rpm, with intermediate mixing with a syringe needle until a homogenous gel-like mesophase was obtained. Samples were sandwiched between two glass slides under ambient conditions, then heated up to the clearing temperature of 55 ◦C and slowly cooled back to room temperature. 2.4. Fabrication of thin film composite PAN UF membranes were used as mechanical supports for the fabrication of thin film composite membranes. A sacrificial layer of PVP was spin-coated on top of the UF membrane at 3000 rpm for 5 min using Laurell Technologies Corporation WS-650MZ-23NPP spin coater. The casting solution for the sacrificial layer consisted of 20 wt% of 360 kg mol-1 PVP dissolved in 50/50 water ethanol solution. The support membrane was dried in a vacuum chamber overnight before it was used for TFC membrane fabrication. Thin film composite membranes were created via spin coating. A membrane casting solution was made by diluting 100 mg of mesophase gel in ethyl acetate at a concentration of 10 wt%. The casting solution was sonicated for a minimum of 10 min before being filtered through a 0.2 μm PTFE syringe filter. The casting solution was then dropped on the support to fully cover the surface and spin coated at 2000 rpm for 1 min.

citation.title: Fouling resistant nanofiltration membranes from self-assembled quaternary ammonium monomers

content: h a 0.2 μm PTFE syringe filter. The casting solution was then dropped on the support to fully cover the surface and spin coated at 2000 rpm for 1 min. The spin coated film was placed under UV light using a Uvitron Sunspot 2 lamp at room temperature in nitrogen atmosphere for 40 min for crosslinking. 2.5. Transport characterization Transport characterization experiments were conducted using 50 mL Millipore Amicon stirred cells (UFSC05001). TFC membranes were placed on top of a polyester (PETE) drain disc mesh spacer (Sterlitech) that was slightly larger in area than the membrane to be tested. A stainless-steel disc with a circular opening of 2.8 cm2 was used to define the transport area. Transport characteristics of the membrane were measured by varying the transmembrane pressure from 20 to 50 psi using compressed nitrogen gas. The setup is shown in Supporting In­formation (Fig. S2). Before transport characterization experiments, the TFC membrane system was rinsed within the stir cell overnight for at least 12 h to ensure complete removal of the PVP sacrificial layer [14]. Data collection commenced after a minimum of 3 h of monitored permeation to establish a well-defined initial flux. Permeance (Px), normalized permeance (PR) and rejection (R) of the membrane were calculated using the following expressions:

Px =VA * Δt * P(1) PR = PP0

(2)

R =(1 -Cp Cf )* 100%(3) Where V is permeated volume, A is exposed membrane area, Δt is elapsed time, and P is the driving pressure. CP and Cf represent permeate and feed concentration. Fouling experiments using BSA solutions were conducted with 1000 mg/L (1000 ppm) BSA in DI water. DI water was passed through the membrane for a minimum of 3 h to determine P0 as the average steadystate permeance. The filtration cells were stirred at a minimum of 200 rpm during fouling experiments. BSA solution was flowed and the permeate mass was measured every hour to determine the permeance. The filtration cell was refilled with additional BSA feed solution if necessary. The concentration of the permeate and feed solutions were deter­mined using a Cary 100 UV–Vis spectrometer using a peak absorbance calibration curve generated from a range of concentrations of BSA so­lutions. 1000 mg/L BSA solutions with various concentrations of NaCl or CaCl2 ranging between 1 mM and 50 mM were also tested for fouling.

citation.title: Fouling resistant nanofiltration membranes from self-assembled quaternary ammonium monomers

content: The same experimental procedure was employed as in non-salt con­taining BSA fouling experiments. pH dependence experiments were also conducted using the same procedure. The pHs of BSA solutions were measured using an Accumet AB15 pH meter. Appropriate volumes of 0.1 M HCl and 0.1 M NaOH solutions were added to feed solutions obtain the desired pH level. Oil – water emulsion fouling experiments were conducted using a 1500 mg/L oil-in-water emulsion. A 1500 mg/L oil-in-water emulsion was prepared by emulsifying canola oil in water using KF-8004 surfactant. 9:1 ratio between canola oil: KF-8004 was used. Canola oil was added into DI water, then KF-8004 was added and vortexed to create the emulsion for the experiment. Experiments were conducted at ~40 psi for 32 h total. The normalized permeance of TFC membranes were compared to the normalized permeance of the PAN support membrane characterized under the same conditions described here. 3. Results and discussion 3.1. Characterization of self-assembled lyotropic membranes AETDAB is a surfactant in which a polymerizable acrylate group and a 14 carbon containing alkyl tail are connected by a quaternary ammonium head group (Fig. 1). AETDAB mesophases were prepared by combining appropriate quantities of the surfactant, a 9:1 by weight

content: Fig. 1. (a). Chemical structure of the polymerizable surfactant, crosslinker, initiator, and solvent (90 % glycerol and 10 % water). (b). Polarized optical microscope images of crosslinked H1 film showing the preservation of mesophase through crosslinking. The mesophase compositions of each sample are tabulated. (c). 1D SAXS data representing the H1 mesophase with varying weight fraction of surfactant. (d). Water filled channels within the H1 mesophase. Columns of H1 phase are surrounded by water molecules, with the quaternary ammonium headgroups formulating the edge of the columns. J. Hwang et al. Journal of Membrane Science 727 (2025) 124101 glycerol-water mixture, hexanediol diacrylate cross linker and 2,2-dime­thoxy-2-phenylacetophenone initiator. The glycerol-water mixture was used as a solvent in preference to pure water due to its lower volatility. The reduced volatility inhibits evaporative solvent loss during sample preparation which is critical for retaining a well-defined mesophase composition and structure. Individual components of the mixture are shown in Fig. 1a. Compositions of mesophases used are tabulated in Table 1. All four variations, A through D, formed H1 mesophases at room temperature as shown by SAXS data (Fig. 1c). The fan-like textures of the UV-exposed mesophases under POM (Fig. 1c) indicate that their H1 structures are retained during crosslinking. 1D SAXS data (Fig.

citation.title: Fouling resistant nanofiltration membranes from self-assembled quaternary ammonium monomers

content: -like textures of the UV-exposed mesophases under POM (Fig. 1c) indicate that their H1 structures are retained during crosslinking. 1D SAXS data (Fig. 1c) shows Bragg peaks of the ordered mesophase for surfactants with different weight fractions. The ratios of scattering vector peak locations relative to the primary Bragg peak (q/q*) are 1, ̅̅̅3√, and ̅̅̅4√. This is consistent with the formation of hexagonally packed cylinder meso­phases, and with the fan-like texture in the POM images. The system shows good retention of hexagonal structure after crosslinking (Sup­porting information, Fig. S1). Fig. 1d illustrates the hexagonal structure of the mesophase, viewed as a cross-section perpendicular to the cylin­ders. The quaternary ammonium headgroups are located on the surfaces of the cylinders or columns and the 9:1 glycerol-water mixture sur­rounds the cylinders. Spin-coated films feature cylinders lying in plane and so the separation of outer surfaces of nearest-neighbor cylinders dictates the transport properties of the membrane. citation.title: Fouling resistant nanofiltration membranes from self-assembled quaternary ammonium monomers

content: PAN ultrafiltration support with a PVP sacrificial layer, followed by UVinduced crosslinking. The PVP sacrificial layer was coated on the PAN support membrane to prevent infiltration of the mesophase into the support that would lead to thicker membranes with reduced permeance. While PVP is insoluble in ethyl acetate, it is soluble in water which al­lows it to prevent infiltration while enabling easy removal during water permeation of the membrane. Fig. 2a schematically illustrates the membrane fabrication process and shows a photograph of a completed TFC membrane. Fig. 2b and c show cross sectional SEM images of the TFC membrane systems before and after the removal of the sacrificial layer by rinsing. A clear thick sacrificial layer can be seen in the cross section before the sample is rinsed, with the color gradient representing the layer of H1 phase and the sacrificial layer (Fig. 2b). In the cross-sectional image of the membrane after the rinsing procedure, the thick sacrificial layer is not observed anymore, and the ~280 nm H1 phase is directly on top of the PAN support, The water contact angle data was measured for the PAN support and the H1 TFC system (Fig. 2d). The hydrophilic head group on the surfactant make the H1 mesophase more hydrophilic than the support membrane. While the PAN support had a contact angle of 50.4◦, the contact angle decreased to 34.6◦with the introduction of the H1 mesophase. 3.2.

citation.title: Fouling resistant nanofiltration membranes from self-assembled quaternary ammonium monomers

content: port membrane. While the PAN support had a contact angle of 50.4◦, the contact angle decreased to 34.6◦with the introduction of the H1 mesophase. 3.2. Fouling resistance of self-assembled membrane surfaces The TFC membrane systems showed high water permeances of up to 6.3 L m-2 h-1 bar-1. Bovine serum albumin (BSA) was selected as the model protein foulant. Proteins are a common type of biofoulant in wastewater streams, exhibiting a pronounced propensity for surface adsorption which leads to membrane fouling and pore blockage that impedes membrane function. BSA is a globular protein with molar mass 66.5 kDa and principal dimensions of 14 x 3 * 3 nm [39] It has a hy­drodynamic size (diameter) of roughly 8 nm in pure water [40,41] and as such, it is much larger than the pore size of the membrane. We therefore expect fouling by surface adsorption and cake formation, rather than direct pore blockage, as seen in conventional nanofiltration membranes [42]. Data from BSA permeation experiments are shown in Fig. 3. The support membrane showed a rapid decline of permeance with the introduction of the BSA solution. Within the first 15 min, the relative permeance decreased by more than 50 %, and ultimately dropped to 15 % of the original permeance. The support membrane did not show a significant recovery of flux after DI water rinsing.

citation.title: Fouling resistant nanofiltration membranes from self-assembled quaternary ammonium monomers

content: %, and ultimately dropped to 15 % of the original permeance. The support membrane did not show a significant recovery of flux after DI water rinsing. The molecular weight cut-off (MWCO) of the PAN support indicates that the pore size of the support membrane is ~10 nm, which is larger than the hydrodynamic size of BSA. However, concentration sampling of the permeate (Sup­porting Information, Fig. S5) suggests that the membrane rejects BSA.

citation.title: Fouling resistant nanofiltration membranes from self-assembled quaternary ammonium monomers

content: This rejection is likely associated with the fouling of the membrane that also results in the significant decrease of permeance observed. Fig. 3a shows the relative permeance of BSA solution over a 72-h period for TFC membrane systems derived from mesophases with different weight percentages of AETDAB. All four membrane compositions demonstrated a relative permeance decrease of no more than ~5 % over the course of the experiment. All four membranes therefore show exceptional resis­tance to BSA fouling. BSA rejection, as determined by UV–vis spectros­copy, is shown in Fig. 3b. All four membranes tested show above 99 % rejection of BSA, which is expected for defect-free membranes with pore sizes in the 1 nm range. There was no consistent variation of rejection with the variation of pore size across the membranes (0.82 nm–0.88 nm), going from 0.65 to 0.75 AETDAB weight fraction. Furthermore, the resistance to fouling of the 65 wt% AETDAB TFC membranes have been compared to commercial Dow NF90 and NF270 materials (Fig. 4a.). AETDAB materials showed approximately 5 % decline of relative permeance over 72 h of 1000 ppm BSA filtration, while both NF90 and NF270 membranes lost over 40 % of their original permeance. While the higher initial permeance of the NF270 materials (Fig. 4a) led to a larger volume of permeate produced at earlier times, by the end of the 72-h experiment, similar total permeate volumes had been produced for both membranes (Fig. 4b.).

citation.title: Fouling resistant nanofiltration membranes from self-assembled quaternary ammonium monomers

content: permeate produced at earlier times, by the end of the 72-h experiment, similar total permeate volumes had been produced for both membranes (Fig. 4b.). The preservation of permeance over the extended 72 h experiment by the AETDAB membrane is similar to that seen in zwitterionic membranes [34] and compares favorably with the antifouling characteristics of a calcium alginate/polyacryla­mide hydrogel nanofiltration membrane, which was evaluated at lower BSA concentrations [43] and against beta-cyclodextrin-modified hollow fiber composite nanofiltration membranes [44]. 3.3. Fouling resistance under differing ionic strength and pH The effect of changing ionic strength and pH was examined. Prior reports highlight the increased propensity for fouling in the presence of salt [45–49]. Increased ionic strength leads to electrostatic screening of the repulsive interaction between BSA proteins. This makes BSA more hydrophobic in nature, and can lead to aggregation, both of which favor fouling. Data are shown in Fig. 5a. No significant fouling (normalized permeance change of less than 0.1) occurred for NaCl concentration up to 10 mM. However, for 30 and 50 mM NaCl, permeance decreased by roughly 0.2 and 0.4, respectively. Similar features are observed in the presence of a divalent cation, Ca2+ (Fig. 5b). Permeance is largely un­changed for 1, 5 and 10 mM CaCl2, but drops by 0.5 for 30 and 50 mM feed solutions, over the 15 h course of the experiment.

citation.title: Fouling resistant nanofiltration membranes from self-assembled quaternary ammonium monomers

content: ). Permeance is largely un­changed for 1, 5 and 10 mM CaCl2, but drops by 0.5 for 30 and 50 mM feed solutions, over the 15 h course of the experiment. Ionic strength scales with the square of ion valency, and so divalent species will exert a greater screening effect than monovalent species at the same molar concentration. Beyond this however, Ca2+ has

citation.title: Fouling resistant nanofiltration membranes from self-assembled quaternary ammonium monomers

content: Fig. 4. (a) Normalized permeance, PR = Px/P0, over 72 h of 1000 ppm BSA solution permeation across 65 wt% AETDAB TFC membrane and Class leading DOW NF90 and NF270 material. P0 was the average of steady state permeance of DI water before introduction of BSA. (b) Normalized permeance as a function of cu­mulative permeate volume of 1000 ppm BSA solution across 65 wt% AETDAB TFC membrane, and Dow NF90 and NF270 nanofiltration membranes. All 3 mea­surements lasted 72 h. J. Hwang et al. Journal of Membrane Science 727 (2025) 124101 additional effects due to the potential for complex formation with BSA. Specifically, Ca2+ facilitates BSA association through its interaction with carboxylic groups in the protein [46,47]. The formation of these ionic bridges between the carboxylic peptide chains results in a denser fouling layer, leading to greater decline in flux. Furthermore, Stronger adhesion forces of BSA to a negatively charged membrane surface in the presence of calcium was reported [48], due to calcium’s favorable effect to the membrane surface. The overall effect is an increased propensity for fouling in the presence of Ca2+ [48,49]. Our results broadly mirror these prior reports highlighting the increased severity of fouling in the pres­ence of Ca2+ ions.

citation.title: Fouling resistant nanofiltration membranes from self-assembled quaternary ammonium monomers

content: presence of Ca2+ [48,49]. Our results broadly mirror these prior reports highlighting the increased severity of fouling in the pres­ence of Ca2+ ions. However, the loss of permeance is significantly less than that seen in data for NF 270 nanofiltration membranes in which permeance losses of 40 % occurred after as little as 30 min at 0.5 and 1.0 mM CaCl2, at lower (100 ppm) foulant concentrations [42]. Experiments conducted at various pH levels showed an absence of fouling at pH 3, 7 and 10. At pH 4.7 however, the membrane exhibited a reduction of permeance of roughly 0.25 after 15 h. The pronounced fouling at pH of 4.7 is because the isoelectric point of BSA occurs at this pH [48]. Under these conditions, BSA exhibits a net zero charge, and therefore loses its electrostatic mechanism for suppressing association. The effect is akin to what would be expected at high ionic strength where strong screening of electrostatic interactions yields similar results. Because of the decreasing permeance of membranes at high ionic strengths or at the isoelectric point of BSA, while some of these membranes were tested for the same duration, less total permeate was treated. This was for conditions which caused significant fouling in particular, which is shown in Supporting Information (Fig. S11). 3.4. Fouling reversibility We investigated the ability to reverse fouling using a simple water rinse.

citation.title: Fouling resistant nanofiltration membranes from self-assembled quaternary ammonium monomers

content: h is shown in Supporting Information (Fig. S11). 3.4. Fouling reversibility We investigated the ability to reverse fouling using a simple water rinse. Membranes were exposed to permeating BSA solutions for 12 h, after which the feed was changed to DI water. Results are shown in Fig. 6. For 30 mM and 50 mM Ca2+ feeds, the permeance recovered to 86 % and 90 % of the original value. For BSA feed at pH 4.7, the water permeance recovered to 89 %. The recovery to relatively high per­meances suggests that the adhesion of the fouling protein layer is not strong. 3.5. Oil-water emulsion fouling resistance The antifouling characteristics were also assessed using an oil-inwater emulsion. The use of such emulsions mimics the conditions of use for membranes used to process oily wastewaters, for example, where fouling by oil droplets remains a challenge [50]. The average droplet size produced was 48 μm ± 24. The emulsion fouling behavior of the PAN support is shown in Fig. 7. The normalized PAN permeance shows a sharp decline of 80 % within the first 50 min, and ultimately reaches a normalized permeance of roughly 0.10 within the first 3 h. The support membrane was subjected to DI water permeation to assess its flux re­covery ratio. However, the flux recovered only marginally, indicting the occurrence of irreversible fouling. By comparison, AETDAB H1 mem­branes demonstrate exceptional resistance to fouling, showing less than 5 % reduction of permeance for all four membranes studied.

citation.title: Fouling resistant nanofiltration membranes from self-assembled quaternary ammonium monomers

content: ison, AETDAB H1 mem­branes demonstrate exceptional resistance to fouling, showing less than 5 % reduction of permeance for all four membranes studied. A simple visual assessment of the feed and permeate liquids (Fig. 7) suggests that oil is completely rejected by the membrane. The results from BSA fouling experiments show that AETDAB membranes strongly resist BSA adsorption. While prior work has shown that the quaternary ammonium headgroup contributes an expected strong anti-microbial function that resists the formation of biofilms [15], the resistance to simple protein adsorption was somewhat unexpected, and its origin is not immediately clear. Conventional wisdom was first built up around the ability of non-ionic but hydrophilic proteins such as poly(ethylene glycol) and poly(acrylamide) to resist non-specific protein adsorption through the formation of a bound hydration layer within which the polymer or oligomer segments swept large volumes. As

citation.title: Fouling resistant nanofiltration membranes from self-assembled quaternary ammonium monomers

content: Fig. 5. Effect of ionic strength and pH on BSA fouling of membranes derived from 65 wt% AETDAB mesophases. (a) Normalized permeance of 1000 ppm BSA solution with different concentrations of NaCl, as indicated. (b) Normal­ized permeance of 1000 ppm BSA solution with different CaCl2 concentrations, as indicated. (c) Normalized permeance of 1000 ppm BSA solution with different pH levels, as indicated. J. Hwang et al. Journal of Membrane Science 727 (2025) 124101 highlighted in the introduction, however, non-neutral charged and zwitterionic species have also demonstrated efficacy in resisting protein adsorption, when deployed as surface-tethered chains, or brushes. One possibility is that the strong resistance to BSA adsorption is associated with the presence of the quaternary ammonium (QA) head­group. QA is a weakly charged species, and as such, its tendency is to disrupt the native hydrogen bonded structure of surrounding water molecules [51,52]. Indeed, in the bulk, QA is a strong chaotrope. As a mobile species, it is known to destabilize native protein structure, the so-called “salting out” effect. The naive expectation is that a chaotropic agent would favor non-specific adsorption, at least onto hydrophobic surfaces, as the display of hydrophobic residues is enabled by the unfolding or denaturing of the protein [53]. This argument neglects several factors however.

citation.title: Fouling resistant nanofiltration membranes from self-assembled quaternary ammonium monomers

content: as the display of hydrophobic residues is enabled by the unfolding or denaturing of the protein [53]. This argument neglects several factors however. It neglects the importance of chemical hetero­geneity at the nm length scale – the pore surfaces feature QA groups separated by hydrophobic patches, at an areal density of roughly 1.5 nm-2, does not account for the confined rather than bulk nature of the water, nor does it include the role of the bromide counterion, which is a kosmotrope. Additionally, the strong flux recovery on DI water rinsing suggests that the adsorbed BSA is likely in a globular form. At a high level, we can surmise that the hydrophilic nature of the membrane does not favor protein adsorption, but it is unlikely that a naive consideration of the known bulk Hofmeister effects of mobile QA and Br-can satis­factorily account for the observed behavior in nanostructured AETDAB membranes with immobile QA groups. However, while the precise origin of the effect remains unclear, we note that the ability of QA groups to confer resistance to protein adsorption has been documented in other scenarios [54,55]. 4. Conclusion In conclusion, experimental data for TFC membranes derived from self-assembled AETDAB mesophases demonstrate significant resistance to fouling under a variety of conditions. When tested separately, the PAN support membrane is rapidly fouled by both BSA and oil emulsions.

citation.title: Fouling resistant nanofiltration membranes from self-assembled quaternary ammonium monomers

content: resistance to fouling under a variety of conditions. When tested separately, the PAN support membrane is rapidly fouled by both BSA and oil emulsions. The fouling resistance of the composite membrane therefore highlights the efficacy of the thin selective AETDAB layer in preventing both the for­mation of a fouling layer on its surface, and the exposure of PAN to the foulant species. The protein antifouling characteristics were maintained at salt concentrations as high as 10 mM, for both monovalent and divalent cations. Fouling could be induced at suitably large ionic strengths of 30 mM or greater, or near the isoelectric point of the pro­tein. Notably however, the membranes exhibited substantial flux re­covery on exposure to DI water. By comparison, the PAN support exhibited marginal or no flux recovery after both BSA and oil emulsion fouling. The antifouling characteristics of these nanostructured AETDAB membranes are attributed to their hydrophilic nature, and the presence of the quaternary ammonium headgroups. Overall, the fouling resis­tance of these membranes and their precisely structured 1 nm scale pores position them as materials of interest for treating organic matter containing wastewaters for which conventional membranes display a high fouling propensity.
